decomposition.title: Project Polaris: Evaluation of Hosting Options (Cloud, DCaaS & Hybrid)
decomposition.industry: Manufacturing, enterprise IT, hospitality, digital innovation, cloud/DCaaS/hybrid hosting evaluation

requirements.text: Vendor proposals are requested to detail target architecture and platform capabilities.
Vendor proposals are requested to detail a high-level transition approach and indicative sequencing.
Vendor proposals are requested to detail the operating model and support framework.
Vendor proposals are requested to detail the commercial and pricing structure.
Proposed solutions should support enterprise-scale deployments, secure connectivity, and integration with Kohler’s enterprise and manufacturing ecosystem.
The evaluation should ensure that application performance and availability are maintained or improved.
The evaluation should ensure that integration with manufacturing and enterprise systems is supported.
The evaluation should ensure that transition risks and business disruption are minimized.
Vendors are expected to propose a target operating model including roles and responsibilities under a shared responsibility framework.
Vendors are expected to propose a target operating model including a governance, security, and compliance approach.
Vendors are expected to propose a target operating model including an operations and support model.
Proposed models should demonstrate how they can support a progressive transition away from the current Data Center footprint while maintaining business continuity and operational stability.
Vendors must clearly articulate the proposed target-state architecture aligned to multi-account/multi-subscription landing zone principles.
Vendors must clearly articulate the landing zone deployment model and application deployment model.
Vendors must clearly articulate environment segregation for Prod/Test/Dev.
Vendors must clearly articulate identity integration with enterprise identity providers.
Vendors must clearly articulate the hybrid connectivity approach.
Vendors must clearly articulate the region strategy and data residency approach.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Data residency appears as a required technical response element in the SOW rather than as a separate legal schedule; no specific jurisdictional restriction is stated in the supplied text.
requirements.jurisdiction_note: Vendor must provide a region strategy and data residency approach; the RFP does not state a mandatory jurisdiction, data-sovereignty location, or offshore-processing prohibition.

requirements.text: Vendors must clearly articulate disaster recovery, high availability, and scalability patterns.
Architecture diagrams, reference designs, and supporting documentation are strongly encouraged.
Vendors must respond for relevant scenarios, including Cloud, DCaaS, and Hybrid where applicable, and clearly state assumptions.
The in-scope services include enterprise applications currently hosted in the Kohler Wisconsin Data Center.
requirements.jurisdiction_note: The current data center is described as Wisconsin-based; the RFP does not state that future hosting must remain in Wisconsin.

requirements.text: The in-scope services include commercial off-the-shelf applications and internally developed applications.
The in-scope services include Windows and Linux virtual machines.
The in-scope services include databases including SQL Server, Oracle, SAP HANA, MySQL, PostgreSQL, and other supported platforms.
The in-scope services include middleware and integration platforms.
The in-scope services include file storage systems and shared infrastructure services.
The in-scope services include backup and disaster recovery solutions.
The in-scope services include identity and access management integrations.
The in-scope services include network connectivity services across enterprise and manufacturing environments.
The in-scope services include monitoring, logging, and security tooling.
Selected OT-adjacent workloads are in scope only where limited to read-only or controlled integration scenarios.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: OT-adjacent workload limitation is embedded in the in-scope list and could be missed by teams assuming broader OT migration scope.

requirements.text: A detailed inventory of in-scope applications and infrastructure was attached in the RFP package online, is comprehensive as of 29 May 2026, and provides counts and sizing for vendor response, solution design, and indicative pricing.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Inventory is an external attachment and can contain hidden sizing or scope constraints not visible in the main RFP document.

requirements.text: Execution of detailed migration implementation, actual workload movement, cutover planning/execution, application refactoring/remediation/modernization, low-level design, steady-state managed services delivery commitments, execution of security controls/audits, tool implementation, end-user training/change management, data cleansing/transformation/archival, and testing execution are out of scope and should be factored in when the contract is in place.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: The exclusions materially constrain what the vendor should commit to in Phase 0 and are easy to overlook because they appear after the in-scope service list.

requirements.text: Vendors must demonstrate capability to host workloads in compliance with applicable industry/regional standards, and evidence of certifications is required.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: This mandatory certification-evidence requirement is in the Compliance & Regulatory Requirements subsection and references unspecified applicable standards, creating a hidden pass/fail risk.
requirements.jurisdiction_note: Applicable industry/regional standards are referenced but not identified.
requirements.cert_required: Evidence of certifications required for applicable industry/regional standards; specific certifications are not named in the supplied RFP text.

requirements.text: DCaaS providers must explicitly provide compliance attestations as per industry standards.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: This is a DCaaS-specific mandatory compliance attestation buried in the compliance subsection and points to the Vendor Response template for more information.
requirements.jurisdiction_note: Industry standards are referenced but not specified in the supplied text.
requirements.cert_required: DCaaS compliance attestations as per industry standards; exact attestations are not named in the supplied RFP text.

requirements.text: Vendors are expected to review and validate the RFP assumptions and dependencies and clearly state any deviations or additional assumptions in their proposal.
Responses are expected to support solution feasibility, approach, and business case development, not detailed implementation commitments, because the RFP is part of a Phase 0 evaluation exercise.
Vendors are expected to propose Cloud, DCaaS, and/or Hybrid hosting solutions aligned to Kohler’s stated objectives and constraints.

Vendors are expected to base their response on the provided inventory dataset, highlight any data gaps or assumptions made due to incomplete information, and use the provided inventory as the baseline for sizing, solution design, and indicative pricing.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: The inventory dataset is external to the supplied document and is designated as the baseline for sizing, solution design, and pricing.

requirements.text: Where data gaps exist, vendors must clearly state assumptions used for sizing and costing.
Responses must be complete, clearly structured, and aligned to each requirement.
Vendors must clearly state all assumptions, dependencies, and constraints associated with their response.
Any optional services or additional capabilities not covered under the scope must be explicitly identified separately.
Proposals must be submitted within the defined timeline via the designated communication channel; the RFP Responses/Proposals due date is 26 June.
Vendors are required to complete the response template in detail; simple Yes/No answers will not be considered sufficient.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: A bidder could appear compliant with a checklist-style answer but be rejected or downgraded because the RFP expressly says simple Yes/No answers are insufficient.

requirements.text: Vendors must provide high-level indicative solution designs and supporting explanations where relevant.
All clarification queries must be submitted within the defined timeline and use the Q&A template provided.
All communication related to this RFP should be directed to Nicholas.Groh@Kohler.com with copy to RFP-Responses-Polaris@kohler.com.
Vendors are required to submit their responses using the Vendor Response template provided as part of this RFP to ensure consistency and comparability across all submissions.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: The mandatory template is an external file not included in the supplied RFP text; hidden mandatory fields may be inside it.

requirements.text: Pricing must be transparent, structured, clearly itemized, and aligned to the requirements of this RFP and the inventory baseline provided.

Vendors must provide a comprehensive cost breakup including foundational setup costs, application and infrastructure hosting costs, migration/implementation costs if applicable, and any consulting, support, or managed services costs.
Vendors must provide separate cost views for SAP and non-SAP workloads to ensure clear visibility into cost drivers across application categories.
All commercial values and pricing submissions must be quoted in United States Dollars (USD).
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Currency compliance is a small pricing instruction that can invalidate comparability if overlooked.

requirements.text: Proposals should include cost increase protections and efficiency gains that lead to a reduction or no more than a reasonable increase over time, based on prices being paid and not general discounts off list price.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Cost-protection expectations are embedded in the pricing section and may materially affect commercial acceptability.

requirements.text: Payment terms are an important part of the proposal; anything less than net 90 payment terms will not be considered competitive, and the proposal should contemplate no less than net 90 payment terms.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Net 90 payment terms are buried in pricing guidance and the RFP states less than net 90 will not be considered competitive, although it does not explicitly say automatic rejection.

requirements.text: Evaluation will use a structured weighted assessment framework, with detailed evaluation criteria, sub-categories, scoring structure, response assessment model, and indicative weightages defined in the Evaluation Template (Excel).
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: The detailed scoring model and weightages are not in the main RFP and reside in the external Evaluation Template (Excel).

requirements.text: Vendors are expected to provide a high-level overview of the proposed program governance model, including governance structure and engagement model, reporting and review mechanisms, escalation framework, and high-level key roles and responsibilities.
Vendors must ensure that all information received from Kohler is used solely for the purpose of responding to this RFP.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: This confidentiality obligation appears in Appendix 8.1 rather than the main response instructions.

requirements.text: Vendors must not disclose, distribute, or share any information with third parties without prior written consent from Kohler.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: This no-disclosure obligation appears in Appendix 8.1 and can be missed if appendices are skimmed.

requirements.text: Vendors are expected to ensure that their proposed solutions are capable of complying with applicable industry standards, regulatory requirements, and data protection obligations relevant to enterprise IT and manufacturing environments.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: The appendix restates broad compliance and data-protection obligations without naming the exact standards, leaving hidden compliance detail likely in later documents/templates.
requirements.jurisdiction_note: Applicable industry standards, regulatory requirements, and data protection obligations are referenced generally; no specific jurisdiction is mandated in the supplied text.
requirements.cert_required: Applicable industry standards, regulatory requirements, and data protection obligations; specific standards are not named in the supplied text.

requirements.text: Kohler reserves the right to modify, amend, or withdraw the RFP at any stage, request additional information or clarification from vendors, and accept or reject any proposal without obligation or liability.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Kohler’s reservation of rights is in the appendix and affects bid-process risk, but it is not a bidder eligibility requirement.

ambiguities: The Key Dates table lists dates without a year; the document cover/version date is 29-05-2026, but the timeline entries themselves state only day/month.

Detailed requirements, response structure, scoring, weightages, and compliance detail are repeatedly delegated to external files not included in the supplied text: the Evaluation Template (Excel), Vendor Response template, Q&A template, and Inventory Baseline dataset.

The RFP states that evidence of certifications is required and that DCaaS providers must provide compliance attestations, but it does not name the specific certifications, standards, or attestations required in the supplied document.

The RFP requires a region strategy and data residency approach, but the supplied document does not state a mandatory hosting jurisdiction, data-sovereignty restriction, offshore-processing ban, local-entity requirement, government-clearance requirement, staffing-nationality requirement, or minimum revenue threshold.
Payment terms below net 90 are stated as not competitive, but the supplied document does not explicitly say that proposals with less than net 90 payment terms will be automatically rejected.

Contract duration, target award date, estimated spend/deal size, and final legal/SLA terms are not stated; the RFP says detailed legal terms, contractual conditions, compliance requirements, and SLAs will be defined in subsequent phases after vendor selection.

Execution services are stated as out of scope for this Phase 0 RFP, but pricing guidance asks vendors to include migration/implementation costs if applicable and consulting/support/managed-services costs, creating possible scope/pricing interpretation risk.
hard_disqualifiers: Submission deadline/channel is mandatory: proposals must be submitted within the defined timeline via the designated communication channel. Exact text: "Proposals must be submitted within the defined timeline via the designated communication channel" [PAGE 8], and "RFP Responses/Proposals due to Kohler 26 June" [PAGE 9].
Use of the Vendor Response template is mandatory. Exact text: "Vendors are required to submit their responses using the Vendor Response template provided as part of this RFP" [PAGE 9].

Detailed completion of the response template is mandatory; simple Yes/No answers are insufficient. Exact text: "Vendors are required to complete the response template in detail; simple ‘Yes/No’ answers will not be considered sufficient" [PAGE 8].

Certification evidence is mandatory for compliance capability. Exact text: "Vendors must demonstrate capability to host workloads in compliance with applicable industry/regional standards. Evidence of certifications required" [PAGE 7].
DCaaS bidders have a specific mandatory attestation requirement. Exact text: "DCaaS providers must explicitly provide compliance attestations as per industry standards" [PAGE 7].
Pricing currency is mandatory: all pricing must be in USD. Exact text: "All commercial values and pricing submissions must be quoted in United States Dollars (USD)" [PAGE 9].

Use of Kohler information is restricted solely to the RFP response. Exact text: "Vendors must ensure that all information received from Kohler is used solely for the purpose of responding to this RFP" [PAGE 11].

Third-party disclosure of Kohler information is prohibited without prior written consent. Exact text: "Vendors must not disclose, distribute, or share any information with third parties without prior written consent from Kohler" [PAGE 11].
compliance_red_flags: Certification evidence is mandatory, but the specific certifications are not identified in the supplied RFP text; bidders must inspect the Vendor Response template and Evaluation Template for hidden mandatory certifications.
DCaaS providers have an explicit mandatory compliance-attestation requirement in the Compliance & Regulatory Requirements section on [PAGE 7].
The Evaluation Template (Excel) contains detailed requirements, scoring, sub-categories, and weightages, but it is not included in the supplied text; mandatory pass/fail items may be hidden there.
The Vendor Response template is mandatory and must be completed in detail; simple Yes/No answers are expressly insufficient.
All commercial values and pricing must be quoted in USD.
Payment terms below net 90 are commercially dangerous because the RFP states anything less than net 90 will not be considered competitive.
Confidentiality obligations in Appendix 8.1 prohibit use of Kohler information except for responding to the RFP and prohibit third-party disclosure without prior written consent.
Data residency is a required response topic, but no explicit permitted/prohibited jurisdictions are stated in the supplied document.

similar_deals.title: Unscoped Cloud/SecOps Automation PoC — Azure Monitor-to-Teams and Qualys-driven remediation
similar_deals.industry: IT services / cloud operations / vulnerability management
similar_deals.outcome: Bid with conditions; win probability recorded as 0.15; final win/loss not evidenced
similar_deals.why_relevant: Partial match to enterprise cloud operations, Azure tooling, governance, support workflows, transition/handover, and commercial content; weak match for DCaaS, hybrid hosting, manufacturing integration, Wisconsin, and global hosting architecture.
similar_deals.key_lesson: Useful reusable pattern is evidence-led baseline, client-owned automation, governance, audit logging, rollback, and phased MVP delivery; however, it is not an exact DCaaS/hybrid hosting evaluation reference.

similar_deals.title: SAP S/4HANA Global Rollout
similar_deals.why_relevant: Closest corpus analog: manufacturing, global/multi-region delivery across Germany/USA/Mexico, enterprise-scale migration/transformation, and strong relevance to minimizing disruption in a hosting transition.
similar_deals.key_lesson: Manufacturing transformation wins required a strong reference architecture, proven enterprise-scale delivery, global rollout model, executive alignment, and low/zero-downtime methodology.

similar_deals.title: Manufacturing IT Transformation
similar_deals.why_relevant: Relevant to enterprise-scale manufacturing IT transformation and risk-reduction positioning, though geography and exact cloud/DCaaS scope differ.
similar_deals.key_lesson: Pre-existing relationship and proprietary IP reduced perceived risk; for Kohler, the proposal must prove differentiated accelerators and risk reduction, not just capability statements.

similar_deals.title: Manufacturing IT Transformation
similar_deals.why_relevant: Relevant because Kohler is a manufacturing enterprise and the RFP emphasizes enterprise ecosystem integration, target architecture, and transition risk minimization.
similar_deals.key_lesson: Strong domain credentials and proprietary IP were decisive against Accenture and IBM; Kohler response needs manufacturing-specific hosting, integration, and continuity proof points.

similar_deals.title: Manufacturing IT Transformation
similar_deals.why_relevant: Relevant to manufacturing IT transition and operating-model requirements; less directly aligned to cloud/DCaaS evaluation but useful for mobilization and delivery confidence.
similar_deals.key_lesson: Fast mobilization was decisive; the Kohler proposal should include named leadership, two-week readiness, and a concrete 30/60/90-day plan.

similar_deals.title: Nordic Insurance Cloud
similar_deals.why_relevant: Relevant to cloud transformation, multi-region governance, data residency, compliance, and commercial positioning, though industry differs.
similar_deals.key_lesson: Multi-country cloud wins depended on compliance, data sovereignty, cost competitiveness, and operating constraints; similar logic applies to Kohler’s global/multiple-region hosting options.

similar_deals.title: Global Law Firm Digital Workplace
similar_deals.why_relevant: Relevant to global/multi-region operating model, governance, support, and data-handling requirements; less relevant to manufacturing and hosting architecture.
similar_deals.key_lesson: Global enterprise deployments require clear governance, privileged data-handling, regional support, and escalation models.

similar_deals.title: Telecom BSS/OSS Modernisation
similar_deals.why_relevant: Relevant as a compliance-loss warning for a complex enterprise IT pursuit, even though industry and scope differ.
similar_deals.key_lesson: A buried local-entity/procurement eligibility requirement caused a loss; Kohler’s mandatory template, deadline, certification, USD pricing, confidentiality, and possible US/local requirements must be treated as bid-critical.

similar_deals.title: Telecom IT Transformation
similar_deals.why_relevant: Relevant as an incumbent-risk analog for enterprise infrastructure transformation.
similar_deals.key_lesson: A strong incumbent relationship can outweigh technical merit; if Kohler has an incumbent hosting/DCaaS provider, displacement probability should be reduced unless a clear risk, cost, or architecture advantage is proven.

win_intel.win_probability_rationale: Estimated win probability is 0.32. This is intentionally conservative. Positive evidence: the deal corpus contains several manufacturing and enterprise transformation wins, especially DEAL-003, a $71M global manufacturing rollout won through strong architecture, low-disruption methodology, global delivery, and sector proof points; DEAL-038/094 show manufacturing IT transformation wins where proprietary IP and domain credentials reduced risk; DEAL-089 shows fast mobilization can win. These patterns align with Kohler’s requirements for target architecture, transition sequencing, operating model, and business continuity. Negative evidence: the only real past proposal match is partial and had a recorded win probability of only 0.15; it supports cloud operations, governance, and phased delivery but does not evidence DCaaS, hybrid hosting evaluation, multi-account landing zones, manufacturing ecosystem integration, or global hosting architecture. There is no public procurement data, so there is no verified competitor award/pricing benchmark and no evidence that we have won comparable Kohler/private manufacturing hosting work. Mandatory disqualifiers around response template, certification evidence, DCaaS attestations, USD pricing, and confidentiality create execution risk. Incumbent position is unknown, and deal corpus loss DEAL-030 indicates incumbent infrastructure providers can be hard to displace. Confidence is medium-low because the assessment uses two intelligence layers: one partial past proposal plus deal-corpus analogs, with no public procurement ground truth.
capability_gaps: No exact past proposal evidence for a cloud/DCaaS/hybrid hosting options evaluation; the only past proposal match is a partial Azure cloud-operations/SecOps MVP, not a full hosting target-architecture, data-center exit, DCaaS, or hybrid-platform assessment. This maps to the RFP must-have for target architecture and platform capabilities.
No retrieved proposal evidence of explicit DCaaS compliance attestations. This is a mandatory disqualifier for DCaaS providers: compliance attestations as per industry standards are required.

No retrieved proposal evidence of required certification packages for hosting workloads under applicable industry/regional standards. This maps directly to the mandatory requirement for certification evidence.

No retrieved proposal evidence of multi-account/multi-subscription landing zone design, landing-zone deployment model, application deployment model, or Prod/Test/Dev environment segregation. These are explicit must-have architecture requirements.

No retrieved proposal evidence of manufacturing ecosystem integration patterns such as ERP/MES/plant connectivity, OT/IT segmentation, latency-sensitive workloads, or shop-floor availability controls. This maps to the must-have requirement to integrate with Kohler’s enterprise and manufacturing ecosystem.

No reusable commercial model was found for cloud/DCaaS/hybrid hosting. The only past pricing evidence is a $182,336 three-month MVP, which is not a hosting evaluation/run/transition commercial structure and does not cover cloud consumption, DCaaS pricing, migration, steady-state operations, exit costs, or volume bands.

No evidence of a Kohler relationship, incumbent knowledge, or Wisconsin-specific/local delivery advantage was provided. This is not a formal requirement, but it is a practical gap because deal corpus losses show incumbent and local/entity issues can decide similar enterprise infrastructure pursuits.
recommended_win_themes: Evidence-led, low-risk hybrid hosting roadmap: use the past proposal’s strongest reusable pattern—baseline-first assessment, no unsupported assumptions, client-owned outcomes—and extend it into a structured cloud/DCaaS/hybrid hosting evaluation with objective workload placement criteria.

Manufacturing continuity by design: position the solution around maintaining or improving application performance and availability for enterprise and manufacturing operations, with migration waves, rollback, OT/IT-aware integration, plant connectivity, ERP/MES dependency mapping, and minimal business disruption.

Clear target architecture plus shared-responsibility operating model: make the proposal concrete with multi-account/multi-subscription landing zones, Prod/Test/Dev segregation, connectivity, security, compliance, governance, RACI, service management, and regional support rather than a generic cloud strategy.

Fast mobilization and transition certainty: reuse the deal-corpus lesson from DEAL-089 by committing to a named team, two-week mobilization readiness, first 30/60/90-day plan, transition factory, decision gates, and executive governance.

Transparent USD commercial model across options: differentiate with side-by-side cloud, DCaaS, and hybrid cost structures, including transition cost, steady-state run, consumption assumptions, resiliency tiers, exit costs, and risk-sharing/optimization mechanisms.
risk_factors: No public procurement data is available, so there is no ground-truth award/pricing benchmark or competitor win-rate evidence for Kohler or this exact type of private-sector hosting evaluation.

The only real past proposal in the knowledge base is a partial match with a low stated pursuit win probability of 0.15; it supports cloud operations and governance themes but does not prove prior success in DCaaS/hybrid hosting evaluation.

Incumbent hosting, infrastructure, or cloud-managed-services provider advantage is unknown. Deal corpus loss DEAL-030 shows a strong incumbent can be difficult to displace even when a challenger is technically credible.
Mandatory response-template, deadline/channel, certification, DCaaS attestation, USD pricing, and confidentiality requirements create avoidable disqualification risk if not tightly managed.
Commercial uncertainty is high because the RFP summary provides no estimated size or duration, and the only past pricing evidence is a small MVP price that is not directly comparable.

Kohler’s manufacturing, hospitality, and global footprint may require complex workload placement, data residency, latency, security, and regional support assumptions that are not evidenced in the past proposal record.

Competitors likely include global infrastructure/cloud transformation firms with strong hyperscaler, DCaaS, and manufacturing references; no procurement data is available to quantify their advantage, so probability should not be inflated.
must_fix_before_bidding: Confirm ability to submit by Kohler’s required 26 June deadline through the designated channel and using the mandatory Vendor Response template; non-compliance is a disqualifier.
Prepare detailed, non-Yes/No responses in the Vendor Response template, including architecture, transition, operating model, commercial model, certifications, assumptions, and exclusions.

Assemble certification evidence before submission: SOC 1/2, ISO 27001, ISO 22301/BCMS, ISO 20000 or equivalent ITSM, cloud partner certifications, regional/data-center compliance evidence, and any DCaaS-specific attestations required by the RFP.

Create a Kohler-specific target-state architecture covering cloud, DCaaS, and hybrid options; include multi-account/multi-subscription landing zone principles, connectivity, identity, security, environment segregation, DR/BCP, monitoring, backup, and workload placement logic.
Develop a transition approach with sequencing, dependency discovery, migration waves, cutover/rollback, business continuity controls, and a first 30/60/90-day mobilization plan.

Define the shared-responsibility operating model with RACI across Kohler, vendor, cloud providers, DCaaS providers, security teams, and application owners; include governance forums, SLAs, escalation, FinOps, SecOps, and follow-the-sun support if global scope is confirmed.
Build a USD commercial model separating assessment, transition, run, consumption/pass-through, DCaaS recurring charges, optional services, volume bands, assumptions, and exit/termination costs.

Validate contracting/legal requirements: US entity, Wisconsin or US support expectations, data-residency restrictions, subcontractor disclosure limits, insurance, confidentiality restrictions, and third-party consent rules.

client_intel.recommended_narrative: Position Project Polaris as a pragmatic hybrid-hosting modernization roadmap, not a generic cloud migration. Anchor the proposal in Kohler’s public priorities: operational and business excellence, modernized infrastructure, data-center operations, cloud platforms, cybersecurity, AI-driven observability, digital experiences, and manufacturing/hospitality resilience. Emphasize coexistence with and clear handoff to Cognizant’s operating role rather than displacement, because Cognizant is publicly positioned as a strategic partner. Recommend an application- and workload-tiering approach: identify what should remain in data center/DCaaS, what should move to public cloud, what needs edge/plant resilience, and what requires managed hybrid operations. Include governance for multi-cloud and Microsoft Fabric/Azure AI while preserving optionality for AWS and Google AI services. Make cybersecurity, observability, disaster recovery, cost transparency, and operational automation core evaluation criteria. The winning message should be: reduce infrastructure risk and complexity while enabling faster delivery of Kohler.com, connected products, analytics/AI, smart factory, and hospitality experiences.

critical_success_factors: Flawless RFP compliance and complete template response are table stakes; one administrative miss can eliminate the bid.
A vendor-neutral decision framework comparing public cloud, DCaaS, and hybrid options using TCO, risk, performance, security, data residency, operational maturity, and transition feasibility.

Credible enterprise-scale target architecture with multi-account/multi-subscription landing zones, Prod/Test/Dev segregation, identity integration, hybrid network connectivity, observability, DR/HA, scalability, and security controls.
Manufacturing-aware transition approach that protects plant operations, enterprise applications, global regions, and business continuity during progressive data-center footprint reduction.
Strong operating model: shared responsibility matrix, governance, security/compliance operations, incident/change/problem processes, service integration, roles, KPIs, and support framework.
Evidence-backed compliance posture: certifications, audit reports, DCaaS attestations if applicable, data residency plan, and regional hosting strategy.
Commercial clarity: USD pricing, net 90 competitiveness, transparent advisory/migration/run cost separation, assumptions, unit rates, optionality, and TCO model.
Relevant references in global manufacturing, enterprise IT modernization, hybrid hosting, data-center exit, and cloud operating model design.
Executive relationship and stakeholder access. Without Kohler-specific context, this becomes a template-scored beauty contest where incumbents and top-tier consultancies have an edge.
A concise but detailed phased roadmap: discovery, inventory validation, dependency mapping, option assessment, target-state selection, pilot/wave sequencing, risk mitigation, and governance setup.
common_failure_modes: Losing on administrative compliance: wrong template, late submission, insufficient detail, non-USD pricing, missing certification evidence, missing DCaaS attestations, or unauthorized third-party disclosure.
Treating the RFP as a simple cloud migration or managed-services pursuit instead of a Phase 0 hosting-options evaluation across cloud, DCaaS, and hybrid models.
Ignoring the hidden Evaluation Template and losing on mandatory requirements or scoring weights not visible in the supplied RFP text.
Failing to address Kohler’s manufacturing ecosystem: plant connectivity, latency, OT/IT integration, enterprise application dependencies, production downtime risk, and business continuity.
Providing a hyperscaler-biased answer that does not credibly compare DCaaS, hybrid hosting, and phased data-center footprint reduction.
Weak or generic target-state architecture: no landing-zone model, weak environment segregation, vague identity integration, shallow connectivity design, and unsupported DR/HA patterns.
Unconvincing transition sequencing due to lack of application inventory, dependency mapping, wave planning, risk controls, and rollback approach.
Commercial non-competitiveness from payment terms below net 90, unclear TCO assumptions, missing migration/implementation costs, or confusing Phase 0 vs execution pricing.
Lack of incumbent knowledge. In this archetype, the incumbent infrastructure provider often has superior asset, dependency, cost, and risk data.
Overusing offshore factory language or standardized accelerators without showing executive-level advisory judgment and Kohler-specific business impact.
deal_fingerprint.recommended_bid_no_bid_decision: BID WITH CONDITIONS. This is worth pursuing only as a strategic foothold into downstream migration/hosting/managed services and only if we can obtain the missing templates, satisfy all compliance evidence, accept net 90 economics, and get stakeholder access. Otherwise, no-bid: a generic response will likely lose to the incumbent or a top-tier hybrid-cloud advisory provider.

Submit a 100% compliant response using Kohler’s mandatory template, with detailed narrative answers rather than Yes/No responses, by the stated 26 June deadline and designated channel.
Quote all pricing in USD and accept or substantially match net 90 payment terms; shorter terms are explicitly described as non-competitive.
Provide hard evidence of security/compliance certifications and, if proposing DCaaS, explicit compliance attestations aligned to industry standards.

Clarify whether Kohler wants a vendor-neutral Phase 0 advisory assessment, a hosting provider proposal, or a pre-sales bid for downstream migration/managed services; the RFP mixes evaluation scope with implementation cost requests.
Secure permission before involving hyperscaler, DCaaS, or subcontractor partners because Kohler information cannot be shared with third parties without prior written consent.
Do not bid a generic cloud-migration answer; the response must evaluate cloud, DCaaS, and hybrid options with a progressive data-center-exit path and continuity controls.
Bid only if there is access to Kohler IT stakeholders through Q&A/workshops or enough inventory baseline detail to build a credible architecture, migration sequence, risk model, and commercial case.

Include manufacturing/plant connectivity, enterprise systems integration, global region/data residency, DR/HA, identity integration, multi-account or multi-subscription landing zone design, and shared-responsibility operating model.
No-bid if we cannot produce credible global hosting, DCaaS, hybrid-cloud, compliance, and operating-model references in time.

Infrastructure cost $7,790.58 uses only provided live unit prices: Azure Defender nodes 100 x $15/month x 4 months = $6,000.00; Azure Defender instances 20 x $0.0202/hour x 730 hours x 4 months = $1,179.68; Azure Defender queries 25M x $0.70/1M = $17.50; Defender EASM 2,500 assets x $0.011 = $27.50; Defender images 100 x $0.29 = $29.00; GuardDuty RDS 250 vCPU-months x $1.00 = $250.00; GuardDuty RDS 200 ACU-months x $0.25 = $50.00; GuardDuty S3 malware scan 1,500 GB x $0.09 = $135.00; GuardDuty EBS scan 3,000 GB x $0.03 = $90.00; Bedrock input/output tokens = $11.90. Contingency is 15% of people plus infrastructure = $54,966.82. Gross margin is 18.5% of sell price = $95,658.01. Total = $358,654.91 + $7,790.58 + $54,966.82 + $95,658.01 = $517,070.33.

Infrastructure cost $2,891.33 uses only provided live unit prices: Azure Defender nodes 50 x $15/month x 3 months = $2,250.00; Azure Defender instances 10 x $0.0202/hour x 730 hours x 3 months = $442.38; Azure Defender queries 10M x $0.70/1M = $7.00; Defender EASM 1,000 assets x $0.011 = $11.00; GuardDuty RDS 100 vCPU-months x $1.00 = $100.00; GuardDuty S3 malware scan 500 GB x $0.09 = $45.00; GuardDuty EBS scan 1,000 GB x $0.03 = $30.00; Bedrock input/output tokens = $5.95. Contingency is 12% of people plus infrastructure = $25,247.86. Gross margin is 15.7% of sell price = $43,886.75. Total = $207,507.48 + $2,891.33 + $25,247.86 + $43,886.75 = $279,533.42.

pricing.competitive_rationale: Recommended Option B is priced as the cost-based price-to-win because no public contract award values or RFP deal-size anchors were provided. The only internal pricing anchor is the adjacent $182,336 three-month Azure Cloud/SecOps MVP. I derived an implied delivery-cost baseline of $51,236.42 per delivery-team-month by backing out a 15.7% gross margin from that past price, then applied a 1.35 scope factor for the larger cloud/DCaaS/hybrid hosting evaluation. Option B's pre-margin cost is $235,646.67, so the competitor-informed PTW band at 1.15x-1.19x cost is $270,993.67-$280,419.54. The recommended price of $279,533.42 is within that band, below Infosys/TCS implied cost floors, and uses a realistic 15.7% services gross margin. Confidence is below 0.5 because the past proposal is only adjacent, no public contract awards were available, and production cloud/DCaaS consumption cannot be priced without workload inventory and provider volumes.

draft.rfp_id: Project Polaris: Evaluation of Hosting Options (Cloud, DCaaS & Hybrid)
draft.executive_summary:
Kohler’s hosting decision is not a binary cloud-versus-data-center exercise; it is a resilience, cost, and operating-model decision for a global manufacturer with hospitality, connected-product, digital, and enterprise IT obligations. Public client intelligence reinforces that point. Eric Tabor’s reported Chief Digital Officer remit includes business-unit and enterprise digital experiences, global data, analytics and AI, foundational platforms and technology, and cybersecurity. Kohler has also publicly positioned Cognizant as a strategic partner across data-center operations, cloud platforms, advanced cloud management, AI-driven security and observability, and Cognizant Neuro IT Operations. Project Polaris therefore needs an evidence-led, low-risk hybrid hosting roadmap that coexists cleanly with current operations, not a generic migration recommendation.

HCLTech proposes a 12-week, fixed-price Kohler Hosting Decision Lab for $279,533. We will baseline current workloads, dependencies, costs, service levels, security controls, and contractual constraints; run a vendor-neutral live benchmark and decision workshop using representative enterprise, manufacturing, hospitality, analytics, and digital workload patterns; and compare public cloud, DCaaS, retained data center, edge/plant, hybrid, and SaaS integration options in USD-only open-book TCO.

The outcome is a leadership-ready decision package Kohler can own: workload placement criteria, target landing-zone blueprint, transition sequencing, rollback gates, shared-responsibility RACI, Cognizant/vendor coexistence model, resiliency tiers, FinOps guardrails, and a contractual transition-disruption warranty. This gives Kohler a clear architecture and operating path to reduce infrastructure risk while enabling faster delivery of Kohler.com, analytics/AI, smart factory, connected-product, and hospitality experiences.
win_themes: Evidence-led, low-risk hybrid hosting roadmap: baseline-first assessment, no unsupported assumptions, client-owned outcomes, and objective workload placement criteria across cloud, DCaaS, hybrid, retained data center, edge/plant, and SaaS patterns.

Manufacturing continuity by design: application performance and availability protected through ERP/MES dependency mapping, plant connectivity review, migration waves, rollback gates, OT/IT-aware integration, and business-disruption controls.

Clear target architecture plus shared-responsibility operating model: multi-account and multi-subscription landing zones, Prod/Test/Dev segregation, connectivity, security, compliance, observability, backup, DR, FinOps, RACI, service management, and regional support.

Fast mobilization and transition certainty: two-week mobilization readiness, named delivery ownership, first 30/60/90-day plan, transition-factory governance, decision gates, and executive steering cadence.

Transparent USD commercial model across options: side-by-side public cloud, DCaaS, retained data center, hybrid, transition, operations, consumption, resiliency, exit-cost, and optimization comparisons with open-book TCO.

sections.content:
Project Polaris must resolve several decisions at once. Kohler needs to know which workloads belong in public cloud, which should move to DCaaS or colocation, which should remain in a retained data-center pattern, which require edge or plant resilience, and which are better consumed through SaaS integration. The wrong answer could increase cost, introduce latency to manufacturing operations, complicate disaster recovery, or create unclear accountability across existing partners.

The challenge is also operational. Cognizant’s publicly announced remit across data-center operations, cloud platforms, advanced cloud management, security, observability, and AI-enabled operations means Polaris should define coexistence, handoffs, service integration, tooling integration, escalation paths, and decision rights. We will not assume greenfield control. We will establish the baseline first, capture known and unknown facts, and make Kohler-owned recommendations supported by evidence.

Manufacturing continuity by design is central. Enterprise applications, ERP/MES dependencies, plant connectivity, property and hospitality systems, customer-facing digital experiences, and analytics/AI workloads have different performance, resiliency, data, and compliance needs. A single hosting answer will not serve all of them. Polaris should therefore create objective placement criteria, segmented transition waves, rollback gates, and resiliency tiers before any migration commitment.

Cost transparency is equally important. Kohler needs a USD-only comparison of transition cost, steady-state run cost, consumption assumptions, DR tiers, tooling, support, exit costs, and optimization levers across cloud, DCaaS, and hybrid options. With this fact base, Kohler can move forward with a low-risk roadmap rather than a platform preference.

HCLTech recommends the Balanced Kohler Hosting Decision Lab: a 12-week, fixed-price evaluation that converts Polaris from an options debate into an evidence-led decision process. The Lab is vendor-neutral and assesses public cloud, DCaaS, hybrid, retained data center, edge/plant, and SaaS integration patterns against Kohler-specific workload requirements.

The solution has five integrated workstreams. First, we create a current-state baseline covering workload inventory, dependencies, environments, service levels, current cost drivers, contracts, security controls, observability, backup, disaster recovery, and operational responsibilities. Second, we define objective placement criteria for enterprise IT, manufacturing, hospitality, connected-product, analytics, digital commerce, and foundational platform workloads. Third, we run a live benchmark and decision workshop using representative workload patterns so leadership sees practical performance, resiliency, operating, and cost implications before selecting a path. Fourth, we produce the target architecture: multi-account and multi-subscription landing zones, Prod/Test/Dev segregation, connectivity, identity, security, compliance, observability, backup, DR, and FinOps guardrails. Fifth, we define the transition and operating model, including Cognizant/vendor coexistence, shared-responsibility RACI, service management, escalation, regional support, decision gates, and rollback controls.

The Lab deliverables include the mandatory template with inline certification evidence and DCaaS attestations, USD-only open-book TCO across options, resiliency-tier mapping, migration wave plan, transition-disruption warranty, and executive recommendation pack. We will preserve optionality for Microsoft Fabric, Azure AI, AWS, Google AI services, DCaaS, and retained data-center patterns, while keeping the recommendation workload-led rather than vendor-led.

This balanced approach gives Kohler a clear target architecture and shared-responsibility operating model before committing capital, contracts, or migration execution.

Our delivery model is deliberately practical: mobilize quickly, prove facts early, and leave Kohler with decision artifacts its teams and partners can execute. We will be ready to mobilize within two weeks of award with named delivery owners, an agreed stakeholder map, secure data-request process, and executive governance cadence.

Days 1-30 focus on mobilization and baseline. We confirm scope, decision criteria, regions, critical business services, application tiers, plant and property dependencies, hosting contracts, cost sources, security obligations, observability tooling, backup, DR, and current incident patterns. Outputs include the current-state baseline, assumptions log, dependency heat map, RACI draft, and benchmark candidate list.

Days 31-60 focus on option analysis and validation. We compare cloud, DCaaS, retained data center, edge/plant, hybrid, and SaaS integration against performance, resiliency, cybersecurity, compliance, data, operational complexity, transition risk, and cost. The Hosting Decision Lab runs live representative workload-pattern benchmarks and facilitated workshops, with human validation, audit trail, rollback criteria, and no unsupported extrapolation.

Days 61-90 focus on target state and handover. We finalize the workload placement scorecard, landing-zone blueprint, connectivity and identity model, observability/AIOps criteria, backup and DR tiers, FinOps model, transition waves, decision gates, service integration, Cognizant/vendor coexistence RACI, and executive business case. We then conduct knowledge transfer and provide runbooks, governance templates, and acceptance criteria.

Governance is built into every phase. A weekly working group manages data, risks, and decisions; a biweekly steering forum addresses trade-offs; and an executive readout confirms recommendations. This transition-factory approach gives Kohler fast mobilization, clear gates, and manufacturing-continuity controls before the first execution wave.

HCLTech will assign a compact senior team designed for decision quality, not volume. The proposed core team includes an engagement lead accountable for outcomes and governance; a hybrid hosting architect for cloud, DCaaS, retained data center, edge, and landing-zone design; a manufacturing-continuity lead for plant, ERP/MES, dependency, and rollback considerations; a security and observability lead for cybersecurity, telemetry, AIOps criteria, backup, and DR; a FinOps/commercial lead for USD-only TCO; and a service-integration lead for RACI, Cognizant coexistence, support, and transition governance. Named individuals will be confirmed during mobilization.

Our relevant credentials are practical and evidence-led. The proposal intelligence available to us shows HCLTech structuring short, fixed-scope enterprise IT engagements that baseline current operations, integrate Azure Monitor, Microsoft Teams/Graph, Qualys outputs, approval workflows, SSH/WinRM execution, verification, rollback planning, audit logging, acceptance testing, and client-owned handover. We will apply the same operating discipline to Polaris: no unsupported assumptions, early proof, transparent controls, and artifacts Kohler can own.

The team will work with Kohler stakeholders, Cognizant, DCaaS providers, hyperscalers, security teams, application owners, manufacturing representatives, and hospitality/digital owners through clear decision forums. This gives Kohler fast mobilization and transition certainty while preserving accountability across a multi-vendor environment.

Kohler should choose HCLTech because our approach protects decision quality before transformation spend. We are not proposing a predetermined migration, a proprietary dependency, or an abstract strategy deck. We are proposing an evidence-led, low-risk hybrid hosting roadmap anchored in Kohler workload facts, business continuity needs, shared-responsibility operations, and transparent USD commercial comparison.

Our differentiator is the fixed-price Kohler Hosting Decision Lab. Before final selection, we will benchmark representative enterprise, manufacturing, hospitality, analytics, and digital workload patterns across cloud, DCaaS, and hybrid scenarios; document certification evidence and DCaaS attestations inline; expose transition and steady-state costs openly; and define named delivery ownership, transition sequencing, rollback gates, and a contractual transition-disruption warranty. This creates a practical basis for executive approval and reduces the risk of selecting a hosting model that performs well commercially but fails operationally.

We also understand the importance of coexistence. Polaris must strengthen, not destabilize, Kohler’s operating ecosystem. Our RACI, governance, tooling-integration, observability, service-management, and escalation design will clarify how Kohler, Cognizant, HCLTech, providers, and internal application owners work together.

Finally, our method is built for manufacturing continuity by design. We will map dependencies, segment migration waves, preserve plant and property resilience, and make rollback and business-continuity controls explicit. The result is a clear target architecture, operating model, and commercial case that lets Kohler reduce infrastructure risk and complexity while moving faster on digital, data, AI, smart factory, connected-product, and hospitality priorities.

critical_issues: The deal-corpus evidence cited for win probability is not present in the raw data. DEAL-003, DEAL-038, DEAL-094, DEAL-089, and DEAL-030 cannot be verified from the provided sources.

The claim that the only relevant past proposal had a recorded win probability of 0.15 is unsupported. The knowledge-base record provides a recommended price and bid decision, but no recorded win probability.

The recommended price of $279,533 is not traceable to the provided data. The only verified historical price is $182,336 for a three-month Azure/SecOps MVP, which is explicitly not comparable to a DCaaS/hybrid hosting evaluation.
The pricing confidence of 0.46 is too high because no RFP sizing, labor model, cloud consumption estimate, DCaaS pricing, migration scope, or comparable public/private contract pricing is provided.
Most competitor financial claims are unsupported by the provided SEC/financial data. Capgemini, Accenture, and TCS financial figures cited by the agent are not present in the raw sources.
The Infosys operating-margin claim is contradicted by the raw SEC data. Agent claims Q4 FY26 operating margin of 20.9%, while the provided SEC data lists Infosys operating margin as 37.22%.

All competitor job-posting and web-search claims are unsupported. The raw data contains no LinkedIn postings, no official Accenture role, no Wisconsin hiring evidence, and no GlobalFoundries/Valmet/TCS managed-services win evidence.

RFP-specific mandatory requirements and disqualifiers, including response template, certification evidence, DCaaS attestations, USD pricing, confidentiality, and RFP pages 7-9, cannot be verified because the actual RFP text/pages were not provided in the raw data.

The killer differentiator relies partly on unsupported competitor-intelligence assertions, especially claims about which competitors have Wisconsin cloud hiring or lack specific benchmark/warranty offerings.
removed_claims: DEAL-003 was a $71M global manufacturing rollout won through strong architecture, low-disruption methodology, global delivery, and sector proof points.
DEAL-038 and DEAL-094 show manufacturing IT transformation wins where proprietary IP and domain credentials reduced risk.
DEAL-089 shows fast mobilization can win.
The past proposal match had a recorded win probability of 0.15.
DEAL-030 shows incumbent infrastructure providers can be hard to displace.
Mandatory disqualifiers around response template, certification evidence, DCaaS attestations, USD pricing, and confidentiality create execution risk, unless supported by the actual RFP text.
Capgemini FY2025 operating margin was 13.3% and 2026 target operating margin is 13.6%-13.8%.
Capgemini is likely the lowest or near-lowest price bidder.
Capgemini has relevant global cloud, hybrid, manufacturing, and hospitality capabilities based on web search.
No Wisconsin-specific Capgemini cloud/DCaaS/hybrid job-posting signal was found.
Infosys Q4 FY26 operating margin was 20.9%.
Infosys has recent Wisconsin postings in Racine, Franklin, Milwaukee, and related SAP logistics/WM roles.
Infosys has recent enterprise IT managed-services wins with GlobalFoundries and Valmet.
Accenture Q3 FY26 operating margin was 17.0% and FY2026 adjusted operating-margin outlook was 15.7%-15.9%.
Accenture has an official Global Edge Cloud Technology Lead for GCP role with Milwaukee listed as a possible location.
Accenture Cloud First web-search evidence supports the stated capability claims.
TCS FY26 operating margin was 25.0%.
TCS has multiple Wisconsin third-party postings relevant to infrastructure, cybersecurity, data science, plant-floor support, and cloud software.
TCS has recent global cloud/data-center managed-services wins.
Only Accenture has official Wisconsin cloud hiring while Capgemini has no verified Wisconsin hiring, Infosys roles are not cloud/DCaaS-specific, and TCS roles are third-party leads.
None of the provided competitor evidence shows a Kohler-specific live benchmark lab plus contractual disruption warranty, except as a narrow absence-of-evidence statement from the provided data only.
Any reference to RFP pages 7-9 as verified source support.
warnings: The raw procurement data contains large federal IT/cloud-related awards, but none are tied to Kohler, Wisconsin private-sector manufacturing/hospitality hosting, these named competitors, or this scope. It cannot support competitor pricing or win-probability benchmarking.

The knowledge-base proposal is only a partial match: enterprise IT/cloud operations/SecOps automation. It does verify the $182,336 three-month MVP and the absence of DCaaS/hybrid hosting/manufacturing/hospitality/Wisconsin evidence, but it does not validate the proposed price or win probability.
The provided financial data includes IBM and Infosys only. It does not support Capgemini, Accenture, or TCS margin-based pricing claims.
The statement 'cloud pricing data validated via Azure/AWS APIs' lacks actual prices, configurations, regions, service quantities, or assumptions, so it cannot validate the $279,533 recommendation.
Adjusted win probability reduced from 0.32 to 0.20 because the positive evidence is mostly unsupported and the only verified proposal evidence is weak/partial.
Adjusted pricing confidence reduced from 0.46 to 0.22 because the price is not directly derived from comparable contracts, public awards, cloud API details, or a documented cost model.
